# Files and I/O {#sec-files-and-io}

::: {.callout-note}
## Core idea
Programs talk to the world. `open()` plus `with` is the foundation; `pathlib.Path` is the modern way to represent a file path; the `csv` and `json` modules cover the two most common data formats. Always use `with`, always specify `encoding="utf-8"`.
:::

In this chapter you will learn to:

1. Read and write text files with `open()` and `with`.
2. Use `pathlib.Path` for file-system paths.
3. Read and write CSV and JSON.
4. Print to stderr, read from stdin, and parse command-line arguments with `argparse`.
5. Replace ad-hoc `print` debugging with `logging`.

We'll demonstrate everything against a writable temporary directory so the cells render without leaving stray files.

```{python}
import tempfile
from pathlib import Path
work = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())
work
```

## Reading and writing text files {#sec-io-text}

The simplest "real" I/O task: write a string to a file, then read it back. Two rules turn this from fragile to robust â€” always use `with`, always pass `encoding="utf-8"` explicitly.

```{python}
target = work / "hello.txt"
with open(target, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write("Hello\nWorld\n")

with open(target, encoding="utf-8") as f:
    content = f.read()
content
```

- `open(target, "w", encoding="utf-8")` opens the file for writing. The `"w"` mode truncates if the file exists. `encoding="utf-8"` makes the bytes-on-disk interpretation portable across platforms.
- `with ... as f:` binds the open file to `f` and guarantees it's closed when the block exits â€” even if `f.write` raises mid-write.
- `f.write("Hello\nWorld\n")` writes a string with two embedded newlines. No trailing newline is added automatically.
- The second block reopens the file (default mode is `"r"`, read) and reads the whole contents in one go with `f.read()`.

The general rule: every file operation goes through `with open(...) as f:` with an explicit `encoding=`. Never rely on the platform default â€” it varies across OSes and locales.

For *line-by-line* reading on a large file, iterate the file object directly. It reads one line at a time, never loading the whole thing into memory:

```{python}
with open(target, encoding="utf-8") as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.rstrip())
```

- `for line in f:` calls the file's iterator protocol â€” yields one line at a time, including its trailing `\n`.
- `line.rstrip()` strips that trailing newline (and any other whitespace) so `print` doesn't double-space.
- Memory stays constant regardless of file size â€” the file holds maybe one line at a time, not the whole text.

The general rule: a file object iterates as lines. Modes: `"r"` (read, default), `"w"` (write â€” overwrites), `"a"` (append), `"x"` (create â€” fails if file exists), `"rb"`/`"wb"` (binary).

## `pathlib.Path` â€” the modern path API {#sec-io-pathlib}

The traditional way to manipulate paths in Python is string concatenation plus the `os.path` module â€” `os.path.join`, `os.path.dirname`, `os.path.splitext`, etc. It works but it's verbose and error-prone (Windows separators, trailing slashes). `pathlib.Path` replaces half of `os.path` with one object-oriented, cross-platform API.

```{python}
p = work / "subdir" / "data.txt"
p.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
p.write_text("first line\nsecond line\n", encoding="utf-8")

[p.name, p.stem, p.suffix, p.parent.name]
```

- `work / "subdir" / "data.txt"` uses `Path`'s overloaded `/` operator â€” the cross-platform replacement for `os.path.join`. The result is a new `Path` with the right separator for the OS.
- `p.parent` is the path with the last segment removed; `.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` creates the directory, including any missing parents, and doesn't error if it already exists.
- `p.write_text(...)` is a one-liner â€” opens, writes, closes, all in one call.
- `p.name` is `"data.txt"` (filename); `p.stem` is `"data"` (filename without extension); `p.suffix` is `".txt"` (the extension including the dot); `p.parent.name` is `"subdir"`.

`p.read_text()` and `p.write_text()` are one-liners for whole-file I/O â€” handy when you don't need streaming:

```{python}
p.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
```

- One call: open in read mode with the given encoding, read the whole file, close it, return the contents as a string.

Walking a directory's immediate children:

```{python}
for child in sorted(work.iterdir()):
    print(child.name, "(dir)" if child.is_dir() else "(file)")
```

- `work.iterdir()` yields each child as a `Path` â€” files and directories alike, no recursion.
- `sorted(...)` gives a stable, alphabetical order (since `iterdir` order is not guaranteed).
- `child.is_dir()` tags each entry.

For recursive search by pattern, **glob**:

```{python}
list(work.glob("**/*.txt"))
```

- `**/*.txt` is a glob pattern: `**/` means "any depth of directories", `*.txt` is "any file ending in `.txt`".
- `glob` returns a generator; wrapping in `list` materialises the matches.

The general rule: build paths with `/`, ask for properties as attributes (`.name`, `.suffix`), and use `iterdir`/`glob`/`walk` for directory traversal. `Path.exists()`, `.is_file()`, `.is_dir()`, `.unlink()`, `.rename()`, `.mkdir()` round out the common operations.

For a recursive walk that gives you each directory's children separately â€” like `os.walk` but with `Path` objects â€” use `Path.walk()` (Python 3.12+):

```{python}
(work / "logs").mkdir(exist_ok=True)
(work / "logs" / "app.log").write_text("ok\n", encoding="utf-8")

for root, dirs, files in work.walk():
    print(root.name or root, sorted(dirs), sorted(files))
```

`root` is a `Path`; `dirs` and `files` are lists of names you can mutate to prune the walk. Reach for `Path.walk()` when you need to act per-directory; reach for `Path.glob("**/*")` when you just want the flat list of matches.

## Reading TOML config: `tomllib` {#sec-io-tomllib}

Python 3.11 added `tomllib` â€” a TOML parser in the standard library. TOML is the format for `pyproject.toml` and most modern Python tooling configs. The module is read-only (there is no writer) and exposes two functions:

- `tomllib.loads(s)` â€” parse a `str`. Returns a `dict`.
- `tomllib.load(fp)` â€” parse a binary file object. Open the file with `"rb"`.

```{python}
import tomllib

snippet = """
[project]
name = "myapp"
version = "0.1.0"
dependencies = ["httpx", "pydantic"]
"""

config = tomllib.loads(snippet)
config["project"]["name"], config["project"]["dependencies"]
```

Reading from disk uses `load` with a binary-mode file:

```python
with open("pyproject.toml", "rb") as f:
    config = tomllib.load(f)
```

Why binary? TOML 1.0 mandates UTF-8, and `tomllib` does the decoding itself â€” opening in text mode would let the OS decode first and could disagree on the encoding. Open `"rb"`, hand the bytes to `load`, done.

A few details worth knowing:

- TOML types map cleanly to Python: tables â†’ `dict`, arrays â†’ `list`, strings â†’ `str`, integers â†’ `int`, floats â†’ `float`, booleans â†’ `bool`, datetimes â†’ `datetime.datetime` / `date` / `time`.
- Invalid TOML raises `tomllib.TOMLDecodeError` (a subclass of `ValueError`) with `lineno` and `colno` attributes for diagnostics.
- For exact decimals (e.g., money, scientific data) pass `parse_float=decimal.Decimal` to keep precision instead of rounding to binary float.

For *writing* TOML, you need a third-party package â€” `tomli-w` for round-tripping, `tomlkit` for style-preserving edits.

## CSV {#sec-io-csv}

CSV looks deceptively simple â€” fields separated by commas, rows separated by newlines â€” but real CSV has quoted fields, embedded commas, embedded newlines, and platform-specific line endings. Always use the `csv` module â€” never parse it by hand with `.split(",")`.

```{python}
import csv

target = work / "people.csv"
people = [
    {"name": "Alice", "score": 95},
    {"name": "Bob", "score": 87},
    {"name": "Carol", "score": 92},
]

with open(target, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=["name", "score"])
    writer.writeheader()
    writer.writerows(people)

target.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
```

- `csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=[...])` builds a writer that takes a list of dicts and writes them as rows. `fieldnames` defines the column order *and* the header.
- `writer.writeheader()` writes the column-name row first.
- `writer.writerows(people)` writes one row per dict, taking values in the order of `fieldnames`.
- `newline=""` tells `open` *not* to translate line endings â€” `csv` handles them internally.

```{python}
with open(target, encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    rows = list(csv.DictReader(f))
rows
```

- `csv.DictReader(f)` reads the first line as a header, then yields each subsequent row as a dict keyed by those column names.
- `list(...)` materialises the iterator into a list of dicts â€” fine for small files.
- All values are strings â€” `csv` doesn't infer types. If you want `int`, you have to convert.

The general rule: use `DictReader`/`DictWriter` when you're working with named columns, plain `reader`/`writer` for raw rows. The `newline=""` argument is required on Windows to handle line endings correctly. Always pass it.

## JSON {#sec-io-json}

When two programs need to exchange structured data â€” a Python script writing a config a Rust service reads, or an API returning a payload to a JavaScript front-end â€” JSON is the universal format. The `json` module converts between JSON text and Python objects.

```{python}
import json

target = work / "config.json"
config = {"name": "Alice", "scores": [95, 87, 92], "active": True}

with open(target, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    json.dump(config, f, indent=2)

target.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
```

- `json.dump(obj, f)` serialises a Python value to JSON and writes it to an open file. Note: `dump` writes to a file, `dumps` returns a string.
- `indent=2` pretty-prints with 2-space indentation â€” readable for humans, slightly bigger than the compact form.
- The Python dict, list, and string values map to the matching JSON types (object, array, string).

```{python}
with open(target, encoding="utf-8") as f:
    loaded = json.load(f)
loaded
```

- `json.load(f)` reads JSON from an open file and returns the equivalent Python value.
- `loaded` is a fresh dict, structurally identical to `config`, but a separate object in memory.

The general rule: `dump`/`load` operate on file objects, `dumps`/`loads` operate on strings â€” the trailing `s` is for "string". Type mapping: `None` â†” `null`, `True`/`False` â†” `true`/`false`, `int`/`float` â†” `number`, `str` â†” `string`, `list` â†” `array`, `dict` â†” `object`.

`json.dumps(obj, indent=2)` and `json.loads(text)` are the string variants â€” useful for HTTP bodies and inline test data.

## Standard streams and command-line arguments {#sec-io-stdio}

A command-line program has three default streams: **stdin** (input from the user or a pipe), **stdout** (normal output), and **stderr** (errors and diagnostics). Keeping errors on stderr matters because shell users redirect them separately â€” `myscript > out.txt` captures stdout to a file while `stderr` still shows on the terminal.

`print()` writes to `stdout` by default; pass `file=sys.stderr` to write to standard error:

```{python}
import sys
print("normal output")            # â†’ stdout
print("warning!", file=sys.stderr)  # â†’ stderr
```

### Parsing command-line arguments with `argparse` {#sec-io-argparse}

When a user runs `python myscript.py data.json -v`, Python gives you the raw arguments as `sys.argv` â€” a list of strings: `["myscript.py", "data.json", "-v"]`. You *could* slice that list yourself, separate flags from positional arguments, convert types, and write your own `--help`. Or you could let `argparse` (in the standard library) do it for you. It handles type conversion, defaults, help text, and validation through a small declarative API. Build a parser in two steps.

**Step 1: a minimum parser with one positional argument.**

```{python}
import argparse
from pathlib import Path

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Process input data.")
parser.add_argument("input", type=Path, help="input JSON file")

args = parser.parse_args(["data.json"])
args.input
```

- `ArgumentParser(description=...)` creates the parser. The description shows up in the auto-generated `--help` text.
- `parser.add_argument("input", ...)` declares a **positional** argument named `input`. Because the name has no leading `-`, it's required and the parser consumes the first non-flag word from the command line.
- `type=Path` tells `argparse` to convert the string to `pathlib.Path` before handing it back. You can pass any one-arg callable (`int`, `float`, your own function).
- `parse_args(["data.json"])` returns a `Namespace` object where each declared argument is an attribute. Inside a real script you'd call `parse_args()` with no list â€” `argparse` reads `sys.argv` itself. Passing a list is the testing/demo idiom.

**Step 2: add optional flags with defaults.** Names starting with `-` or `--` are *optional* flags; users skip them and the parser uses your default:

```{python}
parser.add_argument("-o", "--output", type=Path, default=Path("results.json"))
parser.add_argument("-v", "--verbose", action="store_true")

args = parser.parse_args(["data.json", "-v"])
[args.input, args.output, args.verbose]
```

- The short form (`-o`) and long form (`--output`) refer to the same argument. The attribute on `args` uses the long name with hyphens turned to underscores.
- `default=Path("results.json")` is what `args.output` becomes when the user doesn't pass `--output`. Without `default`, the value is `None`.
- `action="store_true"` declares a pure on/off flag â€” no value follows it. `-v` present â†’ `args.verbose = True`; absent â†’ `False`.
- In our call we passed `-v` but not `-o`, so `verbose` is `True` and `output` falls back to the default.

You also get `--help` for free: running `python script.py --help` prints the description, every argument's name and help text, and exits. Always use `argparse` for any script with more than one argument â€” the cost is small and the help output ships you a usage doc you'd otherwise write by hand.

## `logging` â€” when `print` isn't enough {#sec-io-logging}

`print` debugging is fine for a 50-line script. Past that, you need three things `print` can't give you:

- **Severity levels** so a benign info message and a real error look different and can be filtered separately.
- **Destinations** â€” stderr in development, a rotating file in production, a remote sink for the ops team. The same code, different sinks.
- **A silence knob** â€” turn off the noisy debug lines in production without deleting them, so you can turn them back on when you need to investigate.

The standard library's `logging` module gives you all three. The mental model: every log line has a *level* (`DEBUG` < `INFO` < `WARNING` < `ERROR` < `CRITICAL`); a *logger* decides whether to keep it; a *handler* sends it somewhere; a *formatter* shapes the line. Build it up in three steps.

**Step 1: log a few messages.** `logging.basicConfig` configures the root logger in one call â€” fine for scripts and demos. The module-level `logging.info`, `logging.warning`, `logging.error` then emit messages at the matching level:

```{python}
import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(levelname)s %(message)s", force=True)

logging.info("starting up")
logging.warning("disk almost full")
logging.error("could not connect to database")
```

- `level=logging.INFO` says "discard `DEBUG`, keep `INFO` and up." Switch to `logging.WARNING` and the `info` line disappears â€” that's the silence knob, no calls deleted.
- `format` is a template. `%(levelname)s` becomes `INFO`/`WARNING`/`ERROR`; `%(message)s` becomes the text you passed. Real production formats add `%(asctime)s` (timestamp) and `%(name)s` (logger name).
- `force=True` lets `basicConfig` replace any handler an earlier cell installed. In a real script you call `basicConfig` once at startup and don't need it.

**Step 2: a named logger per module.** In real code, you don't use the module-level `logging.info`. You ask for a logger by name and call methods on it. The name is conventionally `__name__` â€” the module's import path â€” so the logs are tagged by component, and you can tune one component's level without touching the rest:

```{python}
logger = logging.getLogger("payments")
logger.warning("retrying after timeout")
```

- `logging.getLogger("payments")` looks up (or creates) the `payments` logger. Loggers are dot-namespaced: `myapp`, `myapp.payments`, `myapp.email`. Setting `myapp.setLevel(WARNING)` quiets every child unless the child overrides.
- The named logger inherits configuration from the root â€” same handler, same format. You only customize per-logger when you actually need different behavior.

**Step 3: log an exception with full traceback.** Inside an `except` block, `logger.exception` writes the message *and* the current traceback. Use it instead of `logger.error("...", exc_info=True)`:

```{python}
try:
    1 / 0
except ZeroDivisionError:
    logger.exception("could not compute ratio")
```

- Same severity as `error`, but the traceback comes for free. In production logs that's the difference between "something broke" and "exact line, exact stack."
- Never swallow an exception silently â€” at minimum, `logger.exception(...)` it before re-raising or returning a fallback.

The general rule: at the top of every module, write `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)`. Configure handlers once at startup with `basicConfig` (or a real config object). Then call `.debug` / `.info` / `.warning` / `.error` / `.exception` instead of `print`. Levels let you silence noisy components in production without deleting any code.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Why this matters
File I/O is the boundary where most real-world bugs live: missing files, wrong encodings, half-written outputs. `with` makes leaks impossible; `pathlib` makes path manipulation portable; `csv` and `json` give you reliable parsers. Replace `print` debugging with `logging` the moment a script grows past 100 lines, and you'll never lose another error message.
:::

## Going deeper

The `with` statement and the protocol behind it are in @sec-with-match-else. Iterators (which is how a file object iterates *line by line*) are in @sec-iterators. The `pathlib.Path` API is built on the descriptor and protocol patterns covered in @sec-pythonic-objects and @sec-protocols-abcs.

## Exercises {#sec-io-exercises}

1. **CSV â†’ JSON.** Write a script that reads a CSV file and writes the same data as JSON. Use `csv.DictReader` and `json.dump(..., indent=2)`.

2. **Word count.** Read a text file and print the 10 most common words using `Counter`. Strip punctuation; lowercase everything.

3. **`pathlib` glob.** List every `.py` file in your project recursively using `Path(".").glob("**/*.py")`. Print each path's `parent` and `stem`.

4. **`with` two files.** Open an input and output file in a single `with` statement. Write the input to the output, uppercased.

5. **Forgotten `newline=""`.** On a Mac/Linux machine, write a CSV without `newline=""` and read it back. On Windows, the same code writes blank lines between rows. Why?

## Summary {#sec-io-summary}

`with open(...) as f:` covers most file I/O. `pathlib.Path` covers paths. `csv`, `json`, `argparse`, and `logging` cover the most common surrounding concerns. The next chapter, @sec-classes-basics, returns to language design â€” defining your own types â€” using everything we've built so far.
